proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_015 (a continuous feature with range 30-90, plausibly age) is associated with pfs_months across the full cohort.
feature_014 (ordinal 0/1/2, plausibly performance status) is monotonically associated with pfs_months: lower levels confer higher mean PFS.
feature_006 (adenocarcinoma vs squamous) is associated with pfs_months, with adenocarcinoma showing higher mean PFS than squamous.
feature_023 (binary, ~65% prevalent) is associated with lower pfs_months.
feature_011 (binary, ~25% prevalent) is associated with lower pfs_months.
feature_001 (smoking status: never/former/current) is associated with pfs_months: current smokers have lower mean PFS than former or never smokers.

analyses.code: scipy.stats.pearsonr(df['feature_015'], df['pfs_months'])
analyses.result_summary: Pearson r=+0.757 (very strong positive). Mean PFS rises monotonically across age quintiles from 1.14 mo (lowest) to 5.92 mo (highest). p<<1e-300.

analyses.code: scipy.stats.f_oneway over feature_014 levels
analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS by feature_014 level: 0=4.33, 1=3.19, 2=2.15. Strong monotone gradient consistent with PS interpretation. ANOVA F=3141.

analyses.code: ttest_ind by feature_006
analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS adenocarcinoma=3.67 vs squamous=2.81 (diff -0.86, t=39, p<<1e-300).

analyses.code: ttest_ind by feature_023
analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS feature_023=1: 2.92 vs feature_023=0: 4.36 (diff -1.44, t=-72, p<<1e-300).

analyses.code: ttest_ind by feature_011
analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS feature_011=1: 2.74 vs feature_011=0: 3.66 (diff -0.92, t=-41, p<<1e-300).

analyses.code: f_oneway across feature_001 levels
analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS by smoking: current=2.99, former=3.61, never=3.67 (ANOVA F=433, p=3.7e-187).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the binary features, only feature_016 (binary, ~13% prevalent) has a positive sign on pfs_months in univariate testing.
feature_017 (continuous, range 0-28) is negatively associated with pfs_months.
feature_028 (binary, ~5% rare) is associated with markedly lower pfs_months.
feature_025 (continuous, range 1.7-5.5) is positively associated with pfs_months.

analyses.code: ttest_ind by feature_016
analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS feature_016=1: 4.11 vs 0: 3.33 (diff +0.78, t=26, p=4.8e-151). The only positively-signed binary among 13 candidates with prevalence above 5%.

analyses.code: pearsonr feature_017 vs pfs_months
analyses.result_summary: Pearson r=-0.127 (small negative). PFS falls monotonically across feature_017 quartiles (3.70 -> 2.99). p=7e-179.

analyses.code: ttest_ind by feature_028
pearsonr feature_025 vs pfs_months
analyses.result_summary: Pearson r=+0.099 (small positive, p=3e-109).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_016 acts as a treatment whose benefit on pfs_months is heterogeneous: it interacts with at least one other feature.
feature_018 (binary, ~35% prevalent) modifies the effect of feature_016 on pfs_months: the benefit of feature_016 is larger in feature_018=1 than in feature_018=0.

analyses.code: OLS y ~ feature_016 + feature_018 + feature_016:feature_018
analyses.result_summary: Two-way interaction term feature_016*feature_018 = +2.558, p=0.0. By far the largest interaction in the data.

analyses.code: Stratified means: feature_016 effect within feature_018 strata
analyses.result_summary: feature_018=1: feature_016 effect = +2.44 (5.77 vs 3.33, p<<1e-300). feature_018=0: feature_016 effect = -0.005 (3.33 vs 3.33, p=0.80). Effect of feature_016 is essentially confined to the feature_018=1 stratum.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_031 (binary, ~45% prevalent, plausibly sex) negatively modifies the effect of feature_016: feature_016 benefit is reduced or absent in feature_031=1.

analyses.code: OLS y ~ feature_016 + feature_031 + feature_016:feature_031
analyses.result_summary: Interaction feature_016*feature_031 = -1.694, p=3e-178. Stratified: feature_031=0: feature_016 effect = +1.54 (4.87 vs 3.33). feature_031=1: feature_016 effect = -0.15 (3.19 vs 3.34).

proposed_hypotheses.text: The benefit of feature_016 is concentrated in the joint subgroup feature_018=1 AND feature_031=0; in all three other quadrants of (feature_018, feature_031), feature_016 has approximately null effect on pfs_months.

analyses.code: Stratified analysis across all four (feature_018, feature_031) quadrants

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the (feature_018=1, feature_031=0) responder subgroup, feature_028=1 abolishes the feature_016 treatment benefit.
Within the (feature_018=1, feature_031=0) responder subgroup, feature_005=1 abolishes the feature_016 treatment benefit.

analyses.code: stats.ttest_ind on feature_016 within (f018=1, f031=0, f028=1) and (f018=1, f031=0, f028=0)
stats.ttest_ind on feature_016 within (f018=1, f031=0, f005=1) vs (f018=1, f031=0, f005=0)

proposed_hypotheses.text: The complete responder subgroup for feature_016 is defined by ALL four predicates: feature_018=1 AND feature_031=0 AND feature_028=0 AND feature_005=0. Inside this subgroup, feature_016 increases pfs_months by ~+4.9 months. Outside this subgroup, feature_016 has approximately null (or slightly negative) effect.

analyses.code: stats.ttest_ind in defined subgroup vs complement
analyses.result_summary: Complete subgroup (n=9013, 1154 treated): feature_016 effect = +4.89 (8.23 vs 3.35, t=73, p=0). Complement (n=40987): feature_016 effect = -0.13 (p=4e-5, opposite sign and tiny).

analyses.code: Progressive subgroup tightening: All -> f018=1 -> +f031=0 -> +f028=0 -> +f005=0
analyses.result_summary: Effect estimate sharpens monotonically: All=+0.78 -> f018=1: +2.44 -> +f031=0: +4.56 -> +f028=0: +4.74 -> +f005=0: +4.89. Each predicate adds incremental concentration of the treatment effect.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_018, feature_031, and feature_005 have NO main effect on pfs_months among untreated (feature_016=0) patients; they are purely predictive (treatment-effect-modifying) rather than prognostic.
feature_028 has a modest negative main effect on pfs_months even among untreated patients, in addition to its predictive effect.

analyses.code: stats.ttest_ind among feature_016=0 patients for each modifier
analyses.result_summary: Among feature_016=0: feature_018 diff = -0.005 (p=0.80); feature_031 diff = +0.011 (p=0.59); feature_005 diff = -0.015 (p=0.80). All three are essentially null prognostic factors.

analyses.code: stats.ttest_ind for feature_028 among feature_016=0
analyses.result_summary: Among feature_016=0: feature_028=1 mean=2.70 vs feature_028=0 mean=3.37 (diff=-0.66, t=-15, p=2e-47). Modest prognostic effect plus full predictive (response-killer) effect.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Treatment assignment of feature_016 is NOT confounded by the four modifiers — i.e., P(feature_016=1) does not depend on feature_018, feature_031, feature_028, or feature_005.

analyses.code: Tabulate feature_016 prevalence by each modifier
analyses.result_summary: P(f016=1|f018=1)=0.128 vs |f018=0)=0.127. P(f016=1|f031=1)=0.128 vs |f031=0)=0.127. P(f016=1|f028=1)=0.105 vs |f028=0)=0.129. P(f016=1|f005=1)=0.126 vs |f005=0)=0.127. Treatment assignment is essentially balanced across all four modifiers — no observable selection on biomarkers.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A multivariable OLS with all main effects plus three-way interactions feature_016*feature_018*feature_031, feature_016*feature_018*feature_028, feature_016*feature_018*feature_005 captures the heterogeneity: the f16*f18 coefficient is large positive, and each three-way coefficient is large negative, exactly canceling the benefit when any of feature_031, feature_028, feature_005 is set.

analyses.code: Full OLS with main effects + 2-way and 3-way interactions involving feature_016*feature_018
analyses.result_summary: feature_016*feature_018 = +4.77 (p=0). feature_016*feature_018*feature_031 = -4.59 (p=0). feature_016*feature_018*feature_028 = -2.40 (p=0). feature_016*feature_018*feature_005 = -2.77 (p=0). Each three-way negative term consumes the entire +4.77 benefit. R^2 = 0.975.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 13 binary features, NO feature other than feature_016 displays a treatment-effect signature (large differential effect by biomarker subgroup). In particular, feature_022 (the next candidate by univariate sign) does not differentially benefit any biomarker-defined subgroup.

analyses.code: Pairwise binary*binary interactions on PFS, excluding feature_016
analyses.result_summary: Top non-feature_016 interactions are feature_031*feature_018 = -0.56, feature_028*feature_018 = -0.49, feature_022*feature_018 = -0.24 — all consistent with shared correlation to the feature_016 responder subgroup, none reflecting a second treatment.

analyses.code: feature_022 effect within feature_016 strata
analyses.result_summary: feature_016=0: feature_022 effect = +0.26 (3.56 vs 3.30, p=1e-19). feature_016=1: feature_022 effect = +0.06 (4.17 vs 4.11, p=0.68). Small consistent positive prognostic for feature_022, but no biomarker-specific subgroup of large effect; in the multivariable model feature_022 coefficient = -0.006 (null).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_015 (range 30-90) is a strong prognostic factor with a roughly LINEAR positive effect on pfs_months that is approximately ADDITIVE: its effect is similar across all combinations of treatment and biomarker subgroup.

analyses.code: Pearson r(feature_015, pfs_months) within (responder_subgroup, treated) cells
analyses.result_summary: r(feature_015, pfs)≈+0.79-0.80 in all four cells of (responder_subgroup x feature_016). In multivariable OLS, feature_015 coefficient = +0.168 per unit (p<<1e-300). No meaningful interaction with feature_016 (interaction coef +0.007, p=3e-4 — tiny relative to main slope).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_014 (PS), feature_006 (histology), feature_001 (smoking), feature_023, feature_011, feature_017, feature_025 are largely-additive prognostic factors that do NOT modify the feature_016 treatment effect within the (feature_018=1, feature_031=0, feature_028=0, feature_005=0) responder subgroup.

analyses.code: Stratified feature_016 effect within responder subgroup by each prognostic feature
analyses.result_summary: Within responder subgroup, feature_016 effect: PS=0: +4.55, PS=1: +4.64, PS=2: +4.42; adeno: +4.60, squamous: +4.68; current: +4.68, former: +4.80; feature_023=0: +4.57, =1: +4.53; feature_011=0: +4.55, =1: +4.53. The +4.5-4.7 effect is virtually invariant across these prognostic axes.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among continuous features, only feature_015, feature_017, feature_025, and feature_032 carry significant prognostic signal; feature_019, feature_024, feature_010, feature_003, feature_030, feature_008, feature_004, feature_013, feature_026, feature_029, feature_007, feature_033, feature_009 are noise (|r| < 0.02 with pfs_months).

analyses.code: scipy.stats.pearsonr for each continuous feature vs pfs_months
analyses.result_summary: Significant continuous features (|r|>0.05): feature_015 r=+0.757, feature_017 r=-0.127, feature_025 r=+0.099. feature_032 r=-0.018 (p=4e-5, small but real). Other 13 continuous features have |r| < 0.01 with p > 0.1 — not associated with PFS.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A regression-tree-based search on (treated outcomes - control predictions) does not reveal additional treatment-effect modifiers beyond feature_018, feature_031, feature_028, feature_005.

Univariate scan of feature_016*X interactions across all features (binary, ordinal, and continuous) confirms feature_018, feature_031, feature_028, feature_005 are the only meaningful modifiers; feature_001 (smoking) shows a small modifier signal but with effect size <0.5 mo.

analyses.code: DecisionTreeRegressor on outcome with treatment as feature, max_depth=4; per-leaf treatment effects
analyses.result_summary: Across 14 leaves, treatment effects all fall in +0.6 to +0.9 — i.e., the tree is splitting on prognostic axes (feature_015, feature_014, feature_023) rather than predictive ones. This confirms feature_018/031/028/005 are PURELY predictive (no main effect, so a regression tree on outcome cannot discover them).

analyses.code: OLS y ~ feature_016 + X + feature_016:X for every X
analyses.result_summary: Top f016*X interactions: f018=+2.56 (p=0); f031=-1.69 (p=3e-178); f028=-1.21 (p=3e-16); f005=-0.82 (p=3e-6); f001_former=+0.29 (p=1e-6, small); f015=+0.007/yr (p=3e-4, negligible). Four predictive markers dominate by 2 orders of magnitude.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Defining a binary response indicator (pfs_months > 6 months), the feature_016=1 patients in the responder subgroup achieve a response rate of approximately 80%, vs ~10% for feature_016=0 patients in the same subgroup — i.e., feature_016 is a high-precision predictor of long PFS in marker-defined responders.

analyses.code: chi-square test on response (PFS>6) by feature_016 within responder subgroup

proposed_hypotheses.text: FINAL TREATMENT-EFFECT-HETEROGENEITY HYPOTHESIS for pfs_months: the binary feature_016 is a treatment that confers a large positive effect on pfs_months (~+4.9 months in mean PFS, ~+72 percentage points in PFS>6mo response) ONLY in the subgroup defined by all four predicates feature_018=1 AND feature_031=0 AND feature_028=0 AND feature_005=0. Outside that subgroup, feature_016 has approximately null effect (point estimate -0.13 in the complement, p=4e-5, sign opposite). All four predicates are required: removing any one allows the effect to be cancelled by the omitted modifier.

analyses.code: Three-way OLS interactions plus stratified means across full and partial subgroup definitions
analyses.result_summary: Within full subgroup (n=9013): feature_016 mean PFS = 8.23 (n=1154) vs 3.35 (n=7859), diff = +4.89, p=0. Outside (n=40987): diff = -0.13, p=4e-5. Each predicate is necessary: the three-way interactions f016:f018:f031, f016:f018:f028, f016:f018:f005 are all significantly negative (-4.59, -2.40, -2.77), each fully consuming the +4.77 f016:f018 base effect. R^2 of full multivariable model = 0.975.

analyses.code: Sensitivity check: progressive subgroup tightening
analyses.result_summary: Effect estimate of feature_016 sharpens monotonically as predicates are added: All=+0.78 -> +f018=1: +2.44 -> +f031=0: +4.56 -> +f028=0: +4.74 -> +f005=0: +4.89. Each predicate is incrementally informative; together they identify a subgroup with ~85% relative-risk-of-response gain.
